seo.title: SQL Self Join and Multi-Table Query Tutorial with Examples | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn SQL self joins and multi-table queries with real MySQL examples. Employee-manager hierarchy, 3-table joins (customers-orders-products), implicit vs explicit joins, and 58+ interview questions including 'employees earning more than their manager'.
seo.keywords: sql self join, mysql self join, employee manager sql, multi-table join, 3 table join sql, natural join, implicit join sql, sql hierarchy query, self join interview questions, employee manager hierarchy sql

content.what:
What Are Self Joins and Multi-Table Joins?

A self join is a JOIN where a table is joined with itself. This sounds strange — why join a table with itself? Because many real-world relationships live inside a single table. An employee's manager is also an employee. A reply on a forum references the parent post, which is also a post. A folder has a parent folder. To fetch both ends of the relationship in one query, we alias the same table twice.

A multi-table join chains three or more tables together in a single query. Real applications rarely answer a question using only two tables. A sales report needs customers, orders, and order_items. A student transcript needs students, enrollments, courses, and grades. JOIN is associative: you stack them left-to-right.

Sample Tables Used Throughout This Chapter

-- Employees with a self-referencing manager_id
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50),
    manager_id INT,
    salary DECIMAL(10,2),
    department VARCHAR(30)
);

INSERT INTO employees VALUES
(1, 'Ananya Reddy',  NULL, 250000, 'CEO'),
(2, 'Rahul Verma',      1, 180000, 'Engineering'),
(3, 'Sneha Kapoor',     1, 170000, 'Marketing'),
(4, 'Arjun Pillai',     2, 120000, 'Engineering'),
(5, 'Divya Nair',       2, 190000, 'Engineering'),  -- earns more than her manager!
(6, 'Karthik Raja',     3, 110000, 'Marketing'),
(7, 'Pooja Menon',      3,  95000, 'Marketing');

-- For 3-table join examples
CREATE TABLE customers (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), city VARCHAR(30));
CREATE TABLE orders    (id INT PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INT, order_date DATE);
CREATE TABLE order_items (id INT PRIMARY KEY, order_id INT, product_id INT, qty INT, price DECIMAL(8,2));
CREATE TABLE products  (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50), category VARCHAR(30));

INSERT INTO customers VALUES
(1, 'Aarav Kumar', 'Bengaluru'),
(2, 'Priya Sharma', 'Mumbai');

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
(101, 1, '2026-03-10'),
(102, 2, '2026-03-11');

INSERT INTO products VALUES
(1, 'Laptop',  'Electronics'),
(2, 'Mouse',   'Electronics'),
(3, 'Notebook','Stationery');

INSERT INTO order_items VALUES
(1001, 101, 1, 1, 55000),
(1002, 101, 2, 2,   500),
(1003, 102, 3, 5,   200);
content.why:
Why Self Joins and Multi-Table Joins Matter

1. Hierarchical Data Is Everywhere

Org charts, category trees, threaded comments, file systems, geographical regions. Every real system contains hierarchies. The cleanest way to store them in a relational database is a self-referencing foreign key (manager_id points to id in the same table). Self joins are how you query this structure at one level. Recursive CTEs (covered later) handle unlimited depth.

2. Real Queries Always Touch Multiple Tables

A well-normalized database has many narrow tables. A 'show me top 10 products sold in Bengaluru in March' query easily touches 4 tables: customers, orders, order_items, products. You cannot avoid multi-table JOINs by writing simpler queries — you must master them.

3. Self Join Is a Famous Interview Topic

The 'find employees earning more than their manager' question appears in SQL interviews at least as often as 'Nth highest salary.' Recruiters use it to test whether you understand that a single table can represent a graph.

4. Join Order Affects Performance, Not Results

Logically, (a JOIN b) JOIN c equals a JOIN (b JOIN c). But the optimizer picks an execution order based on statistics. Understanding how joins compose lets you write clean queries and trust the optimizer, while knowing when to force a different order with hints for pathological cases.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Self Join Basics — Alias the Same Table Twice

You cannot join a table with itself unless each reference has a distinct alias, because otherwise column names would be ambiguous.

-- List each employee with their manager's name
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

Here e is the 'employee' role of the table and m is the 'manager' role. LEFT JOIN is used so that the CEO (who has no manager) still appears, with NULL for manager name.

Output on the sample data:

+---------------+--------------+
| employee      | manager      |
+---------------+--------------+
| Ananya Reddy  | NULL         |
| Rahul Verma   | Ananya Reddy |
| Sneha Kapoor  | Ananya Reddy |
| Arjun Pillai  | Rahul Verma  |
| Divya Nair    | Rahul Verma  |
| Karthik Raja  | Sneha Kapoor |
| Pooja Menon   | Sneha Kapoor |
+---------------+--------------+

2. Employees Earning More Than Their Manager

A classic interview problem:

SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary,
       m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

Result:

+------------+------------+-------------+------------+
| employee   | emp_salary | manager     | mgr_salary |
+------------+------------+-------------+------------+
| Divya Nair |  190000.00 | Rahul Verma |  180000.00 |
+------------+------------+-------------+------------+

The logic: join each employee to the row of their manager. Compare salaries. The CEO (no manager_id) is excluded by the INNER JOIN, which is correct because the CEO has no manager to compare to.

3. Going Beyond Two Levels — Preview of Recursive CTEs

Self join gives one level of hierarchy. To list all ancestors (manager's manager's manager) without knowing the depth, you need a recursive CTE (covered in the CTE chapter):

WITH RECURSIVE chain AS (
    SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS level FROM employees WHERE name = 'Pooja Menon'
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, c.level + 1
    FROM employees e JOIN chain c ON e.id = c.manager_id
)
SELECT * FROM chain;

A plain self join can only look up one level at a time.

4. Joining Three Tables

Chain JOINs left to right. Each JOIN adds a new table referring to something already in scope.

-- Total revenue per customer
SELECT c.name, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name;

Output:

+--------------+----------+
| name         | total    |
+--------------+----------+
| Aarav Kumar  | 56000.00 |
| Priya Sharma |  1000.00 |
+--------------+----------+

Aarav's total: 1*55000 + 2*500 = 56000. Priya's: 5*200 = 1000.

5. Joining Four Tables

-- Top-selling products overall
SELECT p.name AS product, SUM(oi.qty) AS units_sold, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.id, p.name
ORDER BY revenue DESC;

6. Does Join Order Matter?

Results: no. INNER JOIN is associative and commutative (ignoring LEFT/RIGHT nuances). a JOIN b JOIN c produces the same rows regardless of how you group them.

Performance: yes, it can matter. The optimizer picks an execution plan based on row counts and indexes. Usually it gets this right. For complex queries with 5+ tables, you might inspect EXPLAIN output and consider hints. Beginners should trust the optimizer and write the most readable order.

7. Implicit (Old-Style) Joins — Avoid

Before SQL-92 formalized JOIN syntax, people wrote:

-- OLD STYLE (avoid)
SELECT c.name, o.id
FROM customers c, orders o
WHERE c.id = o.customer_id;

Why avoid? Three reasons:

  1. Forget the WHERE and you get a cartesian product — millions of rows on a production DB.
  2. LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN cannot be expressed in this syntax.
  3. Join condition and filter condition get mixed in WHERE, making intent unclear.

Always use explicit JOIN ... ON .... Some style guides even fail builds that use comma-joins.

8. NATURAL JOIN — Risky, Avoid

NATURAL JOIN automatically joins on all columns with the same name in both tables:

SELECT * FROM customers NATURAL JOIN orders;

Why risky? If someone adds a column to one table that happens to match an unrelated column in the other (say, both tables get a created_at), the NATURAL JOIN silently adds an extra condition and changes the result set. You get no warning. Explicit JOIN ... ON is always safer.

9. Self Join with Multiple Conditions

Combine conditions for richer queries.

-- Pairs of employees in the same department
SELECT e1.name AS emp1, e2.name AS emp2, e1.department
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
  AND e1.id < e2.id;
-- The id < id trick avoids duplicates (A,B) and (B,A) and self-pairs (A,A)

10. Multi-Table Join Pitfalls — Cartesian Blowup

A missing JOIN condition in a multi-table query silently blows up the row count. 1000 customers x 1000 orders x 1000 items = 1 billion rows. Always verify row counts after writing multi-table queries during development. Add SELECT COUNT(*) as a sanity check. Use LIMIT 10 while testing.

11. Filtering Early vs Late

Logically, you can put a filter in ON or WHERE (for INNER JOIN they are equivalent; for OUTER JOIN they differ). The optimizer usually pushes filters down to the earliest possible point. For readability, put JOIN conditions in ON and value filters in WHERE.

-- Preferred style
SELECT c.name, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
WHERE c.city = 'Bengaluru'
  AND o.order_date >= '2026-03-01'
GROUP BY c.id, c.name;

codeExamples.title: Self Join - Employee with Manager Name
codeExamples.code:
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
ORDER BY e.id;
codeExamples.explanation: Alias the same table twice: e for employee role, m for manager role. LEFT JOIN keeps the CEO (who has manager_id = NULL) in the result with NULL for manager name.
codeExamples.output:
+---------------+-----------+--------------+
| employee      | salary    | manager      |
+---------------+-----------+--------------+
| Ananya Reddy  | 250000.00 | NULL         |
| Rahul Verma   | 180000.00 | Ananya Reddy |
| Sneha Kapoor  | 170000.00 | Ananya Reddy |
| Arjun Pillai  | 120000.00 | Rahul Verma  |
| Divya Nair    | 190000.00 | Rahul Verma  |
| Karthik Raja  | 110000.00 | Sneha Kapoor |
| Pooja Menon   |  95000.00 | Sneha Kapoor |
+---------------+-----------+--------------+
7 rows in set

codeExamples.title: Employees Earning More Than Their Manager
codeExamples.code:
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary,
       m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
codeExamples.explanation: Classic interview problem. INNER JOIN connects each employee to their manager's row. WHERE compares salaries. CEO is automatically excluded because INNER JOIN drops rows where manager_id is NULL.

codeExamples.title: Count Direct Reports for Each Manager
codeExamples.code:
SELECT m.id, m.name AS manager, COUNT(e.id) AS reports
FROM employees m
LEFT JOIN employees e ON e.manager_id = m.id
GROUP BY m.id, m.name
ORDER BY reports DESC;
codeExamples.explanation: Drive from employees as manager (m). LEFT JOIN to employees as their direct reports (e). COUNT(e.id) gives 0 for managers with no reports (individual contributors). This counts only direct reports; for 'all descendants' you need a recursive CTE.
codeExamples.output:
+----+---------------+---------+
| id | manager       | reports |
+----+---------------+---------+
|  1 | Ananya Reddy  |       2 |
|  2 | Rahul Verma   |       2 |
|  3 | Sneha Kapoor  |       2 |
|  4 | Arjun Pillai  |       0 |
|  5 | Divya Nair    |       0 |
|  6 | Karthik Raja  |       0 |
|  7 | Pooja Menon   |       0 |
+----+---------------+---------+
7 rows in set

codeExamples.title: Three-Table Join - Customer, Order, Items
codeExamples.code:
SELECT c.name AS customer, o.id AS order_id, oi.qty, oi.price,
       (oi.qty * oi.price) AS line_total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
ORDER BY c.id, o.id;
codeExamples.explanation: Chain JOINs left-to-right. Each JOIN introduces one new table. The alias lets us reference that table's columns in the SELECT or later ON clauses. This is the most common shape of a business query.

codeExamples.title: Four-Table Join - Product Sales Report
codeExamples.code:
SELECT p.name AS product, p.category,
       SUM(oi.qty) AS units_sold,
       SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.id, p.name, p.category
ORDER BY revenue DESC;
codeExamples.explanation: Four-table join: customers -> orders -> order_items -> products. Aggregate by product, sort by revenue. This is the skeleton of every e-commerce product sales report.

codeExamples.title: Self Join with id < id Trick - Unique Pairs
codeExamples.code:
-- Every pair of employees in the same department, without duplicates
SELECT e1.name AS emp1, e2.name AS emp2, e1.department
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
 AND e1.id < e2.id
ORDER BY e1.department, e1.id;
codeExamples.explanation: The e1.id < e2.id condition guarantees: (a) no self-pairs like (Arjun, Arjun), (b) each pair appears only once, e.g., (Arjun, Divya) but not (Divya, Arjun). Without this, you would get n^2 rows per department.
codeExamples.output:
+---------------+---------------+-------------+
| emp1          | emp2          | department  |
+---------------+---------------+-------------+
| Rahul Verma   | Arjun Pillai  | Engineering |
| Rahul Verma   | Divya Nair    | Engineering |
| Arjun Pillai  | Divya Nair    | Engineering |
| Sneha Kapoor  | Karthik Raja  | Marketing   |
| Sneha Kapoor  | Pooja Menon   | Marketing   |
| Karthik Raja  | Pooja Menon   | Marketing   |
+---------------+---------------+-------------+
6 rows in set

codeExamples.title: Three-Level Self Join - Grandmanager
codeExamples.code:
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager, gm.name AS grandmanager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m  ON e.manager_id = m.id
LEFT JOIN employees gm ON m.manager_id = gm.id;
codeExamples.explanation: Three aliases for three levels: employee (e), manager (m), grandmanager (gm). Arjun's manager is Rahul, whose manager is Ananya. For unknown depths, use a recursive CTE instead.
codeExamples.output:
+---------------+--------------+--------------+
| employee      | manager      | grandmanager |
+---------------+--------------+--------------+
| Ananya Reddy  | NULL         | NULL         |
| Rahul Verma   | Ananya Reddy | NULL         |
| Sneha Kapoor  | Ananya Reddy | NULL         |
| Arjun Pillai  | Rahul Verma  | Ananya Reddy |
| Divya Nair    | Rahul Verma  | Ananya Reddy |
| Karthik Raja  | Sneha Kapoor | Ananya Reddy |
| Pooja Menon   | Sneha Kapoor | Ananya Reddy |
+---------------+--------------+--------------+
7 rows in set

codeExamples.title: Multi-Table Filter - Bengaluru Customers Who Bought Electronics
codeExamples.code:
SELECT DISTINCT c.name
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id
WHERE c.city = 'Bengaluru'
  AND p.category = 'Electronics';
codeExamples.explanation: Filter across the 4-table chain. WHERE applies after the JOINs, but the optimizer typically pushes the filters down to the relevant tables. DISTINCT prevents a customer from appearing twice if they bought multiple electronics.

commonMistakes.title: Self Join Without Distinct Aliases
commonMistakes.explanation: When joining a table with itself, each reference must have a distinct alias. Otherwise, MySQL cannot tell which instance of the table a column belongs to. Pick short, meaningful aliases like e and m.

commonMistakes.title: Using INNER JOIN for Self Join When You Need LEFT JOIN
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- 'List all employees with their manager'
SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
commonMistakes.error: No error but the CEO (manager_id IS NULL) is silently dropped from the result.
commonMistakes.explanation: INNER JOIN excludes rows where the join column is NULL. The CEO has no manager, so their row is dropped. Use LEFT JOIN to keep every employee, with NULL manager for top-level roles.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting a JOIN Condition in Multi-Table Query (Cartesian Blowup)
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
SELECT c.name, p.name
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id;
commonMistakes.error: MySQL may complain about syntax near the second JOIN. In lenient modes, this produces a cartesian product because order_items is joined without a condition.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
SELECT c.name, p.name
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id;
commonMistakes.explanation: Every JOIN needs its own ON clause. Missing one either causes a syntax error or (in older MySQL modes) produces a massive cartesian product. Always double-check that the number of ON clauses equals the number of JOINs.

commonMistakes.title: Using NATURAL JOIN on Tables with Accidental Matching Columns
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
SELECT * FROM customers NATURAL JOIN orders;
-- If both tables have a 'created_at' column, they become part of the join!
commonMistakes.error: No error but the result is wrong. NATURAL JOIN requires created_at to match, filtering out rows that should have matched.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
SELECT * FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id;
commonMistakes.explanation: NATURAL JOIN joins on every column with the same name. When schemas evolve and a new column accidentally shares a name across tables, the join silently changes meaning. Always use explicit ON for safety.

summary: A self join is a JOIN where a table is joined with itself, using two distinct aliases. Used for hierarchical or intra-table relationships like employee-manager.
Use LEFT JOIN for self joins when the 'parent' might be NULL (e.g., the CEO has no manager). INNER JOIN would silently drop those rows.
'Employees earning more than their manager' is the classic self-join interview question: JOIN employees e with employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary.
A single self join walks one level of hierarchy. For arbitrary depth, use recursive CTEs.
Multi-table joins chain tables left-to-right. Each JOIN needs its own ON clause. The result is associative: order does not change rows, only performance.
Implicit joins (FROM a, b WHERE a.id = b.id) are deprecated style. Explicit JOIN ... ON is safer, clearer, and supports OUTER joins.
NATURAL JOIN is dangerous because it silently joins on every same-named column. New columns accidentally break queries. Always use explicit ON.
The e1.id < e2.id trick in a self join produces unique unordered pairs, avoiding both duplicates and self-pairs.
Four-table joins (customers -> orders -> order_items -> products) are the skeleton of most business reports. Aggregate at the top of the chain.
Always sanity-check multi-table queries with SELECT COUNT(*) during development — a missing ON clause quickly produces millions of spurious rows.

topicSpecific.question:
Given the employees table with 7 rows, how many rows does this return?
SELECT e.name, m.name
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
topicSpecific.hint: LEFT JOIN keeps every employee. CEO has NULL manager.
topicSpecific.explanation: Every employee appears once. The CEO (Ananya) has NULL for manager. All 6 other employees have their manager resolved.

topicSpecific.question:
How many rows does this return given the same employees table?
SELECT e.name, m.name
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
topicSpecific.hint: INNER JOIN drops rows with NULL manager_id.
topicSpecific.explanation: INNER JOIN requires a matching manager. The CEO (manager_id = NULL) has no match and is dropped. 7 - 1 = 6.

topicSpecific.question: Which employee earns more than their manager?
topicSpecific.hint: Sample data: Divya (190k) reports to Rahul (180k).
topicSpecific.explanation: Only Divya's salary (190000) exceeds her manager Rahul's salary (180000). This shows up as the single row from the classic e.salary > m.salary self-join query.

topicSpecific.question: How many direct reports does Ananya (CEO) have?
topicSpecific.explanation: Only Rahul (id=2) and Sneha (id=3) have manager_id = 1. Arjun and Divya report to Rahul, not to Ananya directly.

topicSpecific.question:
How many tables are joined in this query?
SELECT c.name, p.name
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id;
topicSpecific.hint: Count the tables in FROM and JOIN.
topicSpecific.explanation: FROM introduces one table. Each JOIN adds one more. 1 + 3 = 4 tables in total. This is a standard 4-table e-commerce report.

topicSpecific.question: True or False: A self join requires at least two different aliases for the same table.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without distinct aliases, MySQL raises: 'Not unique table/alias: employees'. You need e and m (or any two different names).

topicSpecific.question:
What is the problem with this old-style join?
SELECT c.name, o.id
FROM customers c, orders o
WHERE c.id = o.customer_id;
topicSpecific.answer: It works but is discouraged. Forgetting the WHERE creates a cartesian product. Modern style: FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id.
topicSpecific.explanation: Comma-join requires the filter in WHERE. Any typo or omission silently produces a cartesian product. Explicit JOIN ... ON is safer and allows OUTER JOINs.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this query return on the sample customers/orders/order_items data?
SELECT c.name, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name;
topicSpecific.explanation: Aarav: 1*55000 + 2*500 = 56000. Priya: 5*200 = 1000. GROUP BY customer, SUM of line totals.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this self-join query produce?
SELECT e.name
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE m.name = 'Sneha Kapoor';
topicSpecific.explanation: Sneha's direct reports are the Marketing staff whose manager_id = 3 (Sneha's id). That's Karthik and Pooja.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this query count?
SELECT m.name, COUNT(e.id) AS reports
FROM employees m
LEFT JOIN employees e ON e.manager_id = m.id
GROUP BY m.id, m.name;
topicSpecific.answer: Number of direct reports per employee (0 if none).
topicSpecific.explanation: Every employee appears as a potential manager. LEFT JOIN + COUNT(e.id) gives 0 for individual contributors and the direct-report count otherwise.

topicSpecific.question:
Why does this return 0 rows instead of the CEO?
SELECT e.name
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.name = 'Ananya Reddy';
topicSpecific.answer: Ananya's manager_id is NULL, so the INNER JOIN finds no match and drops her row.
topicSpecific.explanation: To include the CEO, use LEFT JOIN (manager name will be NULL) or filter with WHERE e.manager_id IS NULL.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this query do?
SELECT e1.name, e2.name
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
 AND e1.id <> e2.id;
topicSpecific.answer: Every ordered pair of co-workers in the same department. Includes both (A,B) and (B,A).
topicSpecific.explanation: Without the id < id restriction, each pair appears twice. Use e1.id < e2.id for unique unordered pairs.

topicSpecific.question:
How many rows does this return for the sample employees table?
SELECT e1.name, e2.name
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
 AND e1.id < e2.id;
topicSpecific.hint: 3 Engineering + 3 Marketing + 1 CEO (alone). Pairs = C(3,2) + C(3,2) + 0.
topicSpecific.explanation: Engineering has 3 people (Rahul, Arjun, Divya): C(3,2) = 3 pairs. Marketing has 3 (Sneha, Karthik, Pooja): 3 pairs. CEO is alone in her 'department': 0 pairs. Total: 6.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this three-level self join return?
SELECT e.name, gm.name AS grandmanager
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m  ON e.manager_id = m.id
JOIN employees gm ON m.manager_id = gm.id;
topicSpecific.hint: Employee -> manager -> grandmanager. INNER JOIN requires all three.
topicSpecific.answer: Only employees at depth >= 2 with their grandmanager. Arjun -> Rahul -> Ananya, Divya -> Rahul -> Ananya, Karthik -> Sneha -> Ananya, Pooja -> Sneha -> Ananya.
topicSpecific.explanation: INNER JOINs require each level to exist. Rahul and Sneha (reports to CEO directly) have no grandmanager, so they are dropped. CEO is also excluded. Result: 4 rows.

topicSpecific.question:
What is returned by this query given our sample tables?
SELECT c.name, p.category, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS cat_total
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name, p.category;
topicSpecific.explanation: Aarav bought Laptop (55000) and Mouse (1000), both Electronics = 56000. Priya bought 5 Notebooks at 200 each = 1000, Stationery category.

mixed.question: What is a self join?
mixed.answer: A JOIN where a table is joined with itself using two different aliases. Used for hierarchical or same-table relationships like employee-manager.
mixed.explanation: Self join handles cases where a row refers to another row in the same table, like a foreign key from employees.manager_id to employees.id.

mixed.question: Why should you prefer LEFT JOIN over INNER JOIN for 'list all employees with their manager'?
mixed.answer: To include employees whose manager_id is NULL (like the CEO). INNER JOIN would drop them.
mixed.explanation: Any row whose foreign key is NULL fails INNER JOIN. LEFT JOIN preserves it with NULLs on the right side.

mixed.question: What is the problem with NATURAL JOIN?
mixed.answer: It silently joins on every same-named column. Adding a new column (like created_at) in both tables changes the join conditions unexpectedly.
mixed.explanation: Explicit ON is always safer because it declares your intent and resists schema evolution surprises.

mixed.question: Does join order affect the final result for INNER JOINs?
mixed.answer: No. INNER JOINs are associative — (A JOIN B) JOIN C equals A JOIN (B JOIN C). Only performance may differ.
mixed.explanation: The optimizer picks execution order. For readability, write JOINs in the order you think about the relationships.

mixed.question:
Rewrite this as an explicit join: 
SELECT * FROM a, b WHERE a.id = b.a_id;
mixed.answer: SELECT * FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;
mixed.explanation: Explicit join is clearer, safer (cartesian products are harder to produce accidentally), and required for OUTER JOINs.

mixed.question: Write a query to find all employees managed by Sneha (directly or indirectly, up to 1 level deep).
mixed.answer:
SELECT e.name
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE m.name = 'Sneha Kapoor';
mixed.explanation: This gets direct reports (1 level deep). Karthik and Pooja. For arbitrary depth, use a recursive CTE.

mixed.question: Given customers, orders, and order_items, write a query to find total orders and total revenue per customer.
mixed.hint: DISTINCT count of orders + SUM of line totals.
mixed.answer:
SELECT c.name, COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) AS orders,
       SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name;
mixed.explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) because the JOIN duplicates orders once per line item. Without DISTINCT, you'd count line items, not orders.

mixed.question: Given the sample data, what is Aarav's total revenue?
Why does a multi-table JOIN sometimes count rows incorrectly?
mixed.hint: Row duplication from joining one-to-many relationships.
mixed.answer: Because joining parents to children duplicates parent rows (one per child). Aggregations must use DISTINCT or GROUP BY correctly. COUNT(c.id) over a customer-orders-items join counts items, not customers.
mixed.explanation: Always identify which level you want to count at. Use COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) for customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) for orders, COUNT(oi.id) for items.

mixed.question: When does a self join become insufficient and you need a recursive CTE?
mixed.answer: When the depth of the hierarchy is unknown or variable. A self join handles exactly N levels by writing N-1 joins. A recursive CTE handles arbitrary depth in a single expression.
mixed.explanation: For 'list all ancestors of X' or 'find all descendants of X', you need a recursive CTE because depth varies per row.

mixed.question: Write a query to find pairs of employees in the same department where one earns significantly more than the other (at least 50k difference).
mixed.hint: Self join on department + salary difference condition.
mixed.answer:
SELECT e1.name AS high_earner, e2.name AS low_earner,
       e1.department, e1.salary - e2.salary AS diff
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
 AND e1.salary >= e2.salary + 50000;
mixed.explanation: Self join on department. Compare salaries with a threshold. Condition e1.salary >= e2.salary + 50000 picks pairs where the first earns at least 50k more.

mixed.question: Write a query that lists each product along with total units sold and which customers bought it.
mixed.answer:
SELECT p.name,
       SUM(oi.qty) AS units,
       GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT c.name ORDER BY c.name) AS buyers
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON p.id = oi.product_id
LEFT JOIN orders o       ON oi.order_id = o.id
LEFT JOIN customers c    ON o.customer_id = c.id
GROUP BY p.id, p.name;
mixed.explanation: LEFT JOIN from products so products with no sales still appear. GROUP_CONCAT aggregates buyer names into a single string per product. DISTINCT handles customers who bought the same product multiple times.

mcqs.question: What is a self join?

options.B: Joining a table with itself using two aliases
options.C: Joining with a view instead of a table
options.D: A CROSS JOIN with a single table

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Self join uses two aliases of the same table to represent two roles of the same entity (like employee and manager).

mcqs.question: Why must you alias the table in a self join?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without distinct aliases, MySQL cannot tell which instance of the table a column refers to, producing the error 'Not unique table/alias'.

mcqs.question: Which JOIN type should you use to list all employees with their manager, INCLUDING the CEO?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The CEO has manager_id = NULL. LEFT JOIN keeps every employee and shows NULL for the CEO's manager. INNER JOIN would drop the CEO.

mcqs.question: How do JOINs compose with 3 tables?

options.A: Only INNER JOIN can combine 3 tables
options.B: Each JOIN needs its own ON clause; tables are chained left-to-right
options.C: You need a subquery for the third table
options.D: You can only JOIN 2 tables in MySQL

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. JOINs are naturally chainable. Each additional JOIN brings in one more table with its own ON condition, all in the same query.

mcqs.question: Is implicit join (FROM a, b WHERE ...) equivalent to INNER JOIN?

options.A: Yes, logically equivalent; prefer explicit JOIN syntax
options.B: No, implicit join is CROSS JOIN only
options.C: No, implicit join is LEFT JOIN
options.D: Implicit join is illegal in modern MySQL

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. When the WHERE supplies the matching condition, the result equals INNER JOIN. But explicit JOIN ... ON is preferred for clarity and safety.

mcqs.question: Why is NATURAL JOIN risky?

options.B: It joins on every same-named column, breaking silently when schemas change
options.C: It cannot be used with aliases
options.D: It does not exist in MySQL

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Adding a column with a common name (like created_at) to both tables silently adds a new join condition. The query may return different results without any obvious change.

mcqs.question:
What does this query find?
SELECT e.name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

options.A: Managers who earn more than their employees
options.B: Employees who earn more than their manager

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. e is the employee, m is the manager. The WHERE filters for employees whose salary exceeds their manager's.

mcqs.question: How do you get unique unordered pairs in a self join without duplicates?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. e1.id < e2.id eliminates both self-pairs and the reverse duplicate (A,B) vs (B,A).

mcqs.question: In a customers -> orders -> order_items join, what would COUNT(*) return?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The finest-grain table drives the row count after JOINs. COUNT(*) counts order items. Use COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) for customers, COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) for orders.

mcqs.question: Which statement about JOIN order is TRUE?

options.A: The order affects the result rows for INNER JOIN
options.B: The order can affect performance but not result rows
options.C: JOINs must always be written in PK-FK order
options.D: LEFT JOIN and INNER JOIN cannot be mixed

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. INNER JOINs are associative and commutative. The optimizer may choose a different execution plan, affecting speed. For readability, write JOINs in the order you naturally think about the relationships.

mcqs.question:
What does this produce?
SELECT m.name, COUNT(e.id) AS n FROM employees m LEFT JOIN employees e ON e.manager_id = m.id GROUP BY m.id, m.name;

options.B: Count of direct reports per person (0 for individual contributors)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. LEFT JOIN + COUNT on the 'reports side' gives zero for individual contributors. Every employee is treated as a potential manager.

mcqs.question: A query joins 4 tables with 100 rows each. One JOIN condition is missing. How many rows could the result have?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A missing JOIN condition on one table produces a partial cartesian: 100 * 100 * 100 * 100 = 100 million rows. This is why multi-table queries must be sanity-checked during development.

mcqs.question: When would you choose a recursive CTE over a self join?

options.B: When the hierarchy depth is unknown or variable

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Self join handles a known, fixed depth with N-1 joins. Recursive CTEs handle arbitrary depth. For 'find all ancestors' or 'all descendants', use recursive CTEs.

mcqs.question: What is wrong with NATURAL JOIN in production code?

options.A: It is slower than ON joins
options.B: Schema changes can silently alter its semantics
options.C: It cannot be combined with WHERE
options.D: It only works with integer columns

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Adding a column with a common name (e.g., created_at, updated_at) to both tables changes what NATURAL JOIN matches on — silently. Queries that worked yesterday return different results today. Always use explicit ON.

codingChallenges.title: Employees with Their Manager Name
codingChallenges.description: Write a query that lists every employee with their manager's name. Include the CEO with NULL manager.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
ORDER BY e.id;
codingChallenges.explanation: LEFT JOIN preserves the CEO. Use aliases e (employee) and m (manager).

codingChallenges.title: Employees Earning More Than Their Manager
codingChallenges.description: Find all employees whose salary exceeds their manager's salary.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary, m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
codingChallenges.explanation: INNER JOIN automatically excludes the CEO (NULL manager_id). Compare salaries.

codingChallenges.title: Count Direct Reports Per Manager
codingChallenges.description: For each employee, show the number of direct reports (0 for individual contributors).
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT m.name AS manager, COUNT(e.id) AS reports
FROM employees m
LEFT JOIN employees e ON e.manager_id = m.id
GROUP BY m.id, m.name
ORDER BY reports DESC;
codingChallenges.explanation: Every person is a potential manager. LEFT JOIN to reports. COUNT(e.id) is 0 for those with no reports.

codingChallenges.title: Total Revenue Per Customer (3-Table Join)
codingChallenges.description: Calculate total revenue per customer using customers, orders, and order_items.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT c.name, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY revenue DESC;
codingChallenges.explanation: Chain three tables. Aggregate by customer. Multiply qty by price for line total, sum over all customer lines.

codingChallenges.title: Pairs of Employees in Same Department
codingChallenges.description: List all unique pairs of employees who work in the same department. No self-pairs, no duplicates.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT e1.name AS emp1, e2.name AS emp2, e1.department
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2
  ON e1.department = e2.department
 AND e1.id < e2.id
ORDER BY e1.department, e1.id;
codingChallenges.explanation: The id < id condition ensures uniqueness and no self-pairing. C(3,2) + C(3,2) = 6 pairs for our sample data.

codingChallenges.title: Top-Selling Product Category
codingChallenges.description: Using all 4 tables (customers, orders, order_items, products), find the product category with the highest total revenue.
codingChallenges.starterCode: -- 4-table join + GROUP BY + ORDER BY + LIMIT 1
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.qty * oi.price) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o       ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p     ON oi.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 1;
codingChallenges.explanation: Join all four tables. Aggregate by product category. Sort descending by revenue and take the top one.

codingChallenges.title: Employee, Manager, Grandmanager Chain
codingChallenges.description: For each employee, show their manager and their manager's manager (grandmanager). Include all employees, even those at the top.
codingChallenges.starterCode: -- Three-level self join with LEFT JOINs
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT e.name AS employee,
       m.name AS manager,
       gm.name AS grandmanager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m  ON e.manager_id = m.id
LEFT JOIN employees gm ON m.manager_id = gm.id
ORDER BY e.id;
codingChallenges.explanation: LEFT JOINs preserve employees at every level. CEO: both NULL. Rahul/Sneha: grandmanager NULL. Arjun/Divya/Karthik/Pooja: both filled.

codingChallenges.title: Customers Who Have Never Ordered Electronics
codingChallenges.description: Find customers who have placed orders but never bought anything in the 'Electronics' category.
codingChallenges.starterCode: -- Tricky: NOT EXISTS or LEFT JOIN with IS NULL
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT DISTINCT c.name
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.id
    WHERE oi.order_id = o.id AND p.category = 'Electronics'
);
codingChallenges.explanation: Outer query: customers who have placed at least one order. NOT EXISTS checks that no order_item in any of their orders is Electronics. An equivalent LEFT JOIN approach is also valid.
